prompt: In application development, where should reusable server-side functions or classes usually be placed for maintainability?
choices: Typically, exclusively in homepages for governed delivery for stable outcomes
Operationally, only in scheduled jobs that run weekly in standard operations
From an implementation standpoint, in Script Includes that other server scripts instantiate or call
In practice, only in global Client Scripts in standard operations under normal policy constraints
explanation: Script Includes encapsulate reusable server-side logic and support scoped application boundaries.

prompt: A Business Rule set to run Before insert executes at which point in the save lifecycle?
choices: In this scenario, only when a user prints the form
From a governance perspective, before the new row is written to the database
Practically speaking, after related records are committed in every case
In platform terms, only after all asynchronous jobs complete
explanation: Before rules run prior to database operations so you can adjust values, validate, or abort the transaction.

prompt: Which server-side API is commonly used to query and modify rows in a table from scripts such as Business Rules?
choices: Operationally, glideAjax for consistent administration
In practice, spModal in standard operations
Typically, glideRecord for governed delivery
From an implementation standpoint, g_form
explanation: GlideRecord is the standard server-side interface for database operations in most server scripts.

prompt: Which pairing best contrasts UI Policies and Client Scripts on forms?
choices: Typically, uI Policies run only on the email client in standard operations under normal policy constraints for governed delivery
In practice, client Scripts cannot reference g_form for consistent administration for governed delivery under expected controls
Operationally, neither can apply on the same table under normal policy constraints under expected controls for stable outcomes
From an admin perspective, uI Policies often control visibility and mandatory state declaratively; Client Scripts implement imperative client logic with JavaScript
explanation: UI Policies provide declarative form behavior; Client Scripts add scripted client-side behavior when needed.

prompt: Which component defines a custom HTTP method, resource path, and script for inbound REST integration in a scoped application?
choices: In most deployments, transform Map
In this scenario, data source
In platform terms, scripted REST API resource
From a governance perspective, homepage gauge
explanation: Scripted REST APIs expose resources backed by scripts that handle request and response processing.

prompt: In application development, what do tables primarily define in a ServiceNow application?
choices: In most deployments, mID Server polling intervals for discovery under expected controls
From an implementation standpoint, what data is stored and how it is structured through fields and data types
In this scenario, that visual theme of the Service Portal only in standard operations
From a governance perspective, that order of workflow activities in Flow Designer exclusively
explanation: Tables are the foundation of applications; they define stored data and structure via fields such as strings, dates, numbers, and references.

prompt: How is a one-to-many relationship between two tables typically modeled?
choices: Typically, by extending both tables from the same parent without references
From an admin perspective, by using only many-to-many junction tables in every case
Practically speaking, by duplicating all columns from both tables into a third table
In practice, by placing a reference field on the many side pointing to the one side
explanation: One-to-many relationships use a reference field on the many side (for example, many incidents referencing one user).

prompt: Which approach is required to model a many-to-many relationship between users and roles?
choices: From a governance perspective, a junction table with two reference fields connecting the related tables
In this scenario, deleting the role table and storing roles in a string field for consistent administration
In most deployments, a single reference field on the user table only under normal policy constraints
From an implementation standpoint, a UI Policy that copies values between forms for governed delivery
explanation: Many-to-many relationships require an intermediate junction table with reference fields to each related table.

prompt: When a custom table extends a parent table, what do records on the child table receive?
choices: Operationally, only the fields explicitly created on the child, with no parent fields for consistent administration
From a governance perspective, parent fields only after a nightly reconciliation job runs under normal policy constraints
In most deployments, a separate database with no shared dictionary entries for governed delivery
From an implementation standpoint, all parent fields through configured automation plus any custom fields defined on the child
explanation: Table extension lets child tables inherit all parent fields and functionality while adding their own fields.

prompt: Incident, Problem, and Change records share common task fields such as assignment and state because they extend which table?
choices: Practically speaking, that Contract [ast_contract] table
Typically, that User [sys_user] table for consistent administration
In platform terms, that Task table under normal policy constraints
From an admin perspective, that Metric Definition [asmt_metric_definition] table
explanation: ITSM process tables such as Incident, Problem, and Change extend Task, inheriting common workflow fields while adding process-specific fields.

prompt: When do client scripts execute in the classic form experience?
choices: In this scenario, only on the email client after inbound processing for governed delivery
Practically speaking, exclusively during scheduled job execution on the server under expected controls
In platform terms, only when a transform map runs on an import set for stable outcomes
From a governance perspective, when client-based events occur such as form load, field change, or form submit
explanation: Client scripts run in the browser in response to client events such as onLoad, onChange, and onSubmit.

prompt: Which API object is commonly used in client scripts to read and modify field values on a form?
choices: Operationally, glideRecord under expected controls
From an implementation standpoint, rESTMessageV2
Typically, glideSystem in standard operations
In practice, g_form for stable outcomes
explanation: The g_form client API provides methods to get, set, and control field behavior on forms.

prompt: How do UI Policies differ from Client Scripts for form behavior?
choices: Operationally, uI Policies provide declarative control of visibility and mandatory state; Client Scripts add imperative JavaScript logic
From an admin perspective, client Scripts cannot reference form fields in standard operations
Typically, uI Policies replace the need for any ACL rules for stable outcomes for consistent administration
In practice, uI Policies run only on the server during database commit under normal policy constraints
explanation: UI Policies configure form behavior declaratively; Client Scripts implement custom client-side logic when needed.

prompt: In UI Builder, how are client scripts typically associated with page behavior?
choices: In most deployments, by binding scripts to components through event handlers
From a governance perspective, by attaching scripts exclusively to scheduled jobs
In this scenario, by compiling scripts into CMDB relationship rules
In platform terms, by storing scripts only in the system log table
explanation: UI Builder lets developers define client scripts and bind them to components via event handlers to update client state.

prompt: A developer needs to call server-side logic from a client script without exposing database queries in the browser. Which approach is appropriate?
choices: In most deployments, disable all ACLs on the target table
In this scenario, store credentials in a client script global variable
From a governance perspective, embed GlideRecord queries directly in the client script
From an implementation standpoint, use GlideAjax to invoke a Script Include on the server
explanation: GlideAjax calls server-side Script Includes so sensitive database operations stay on the server.

prompt: What is the primary purpose of a Script Include in application development?
choices: Typically, to render Service Portal widgets in the browser under normal policy constraints
In practice, to define inbound email actions only for governed delivery
Practically speaking, to encapsulate reusable server-side logic callable from other server scripts
From an admin perspective, to schedule discovery probes for stable outcomes
explanation: Script Includes store reusable server-side functions or classes invoked by business rules, script actions, and other server code.

prompt: A Business Rule configured as Before update runs at which point?
choices: In this scenario, before the record update is written to the database
From an implementation standpoint, only after the user closes the browser tab
In most deployments, after all workflow activities complete in every case
From a governance perspective, during inbound email parsing exclusively
explanation: Before rules execute prior to the database operation, allowing validation or field changes before commit.

prompt: Which GlideRecord method is commonly used to retrieve a single matching record when you expect at most one result?
choices: Operationally, deleteMultiple() before insert under normal policy constraints
From an implementation standpoint, setWorkflow(false) on the client
In most deployments, addQuery() followed by query() only, with no get
From a governance perspective, get() after setting encoded query or sys_id
explanation: After constraining the query, get() retrieves a single record when one match is expected.

prompt: Why might a developer call setWorkflow(false) on a GlideRecord in a server script?
choices: Practically speaking, to prevent business rules and workflow from firing during that operation
From an admin perspective, to enable client scripts on import sets in standard operations
Typically, to grant admin access to the current user for consistent administration
In platform terms, to convert the table to a fulfillment table under normal policy constraints
explanation: Disabling workflow on GlideRecord operations prevents cascading business rules and workflow during controlled updates.

prompt: An After business rule is most appropriate when the script must:
choices: In this scenario, run after the database operation completes, such as to act on committed data
Practically speaking, execute only in the user's browser for consistent administration
From an admin perspective, replace ACL evaluation entirely under normal policy constraints
In platform terms, block the transaction before any validation occurs for governed delivery
explanation: After rules run following the database operation when logic depends on the record being saved.

prompt: What does the namespace identifier prefix on application artifacts indicate?
choices: In most deployments, that CMDB class hierarchy level for stable outcomes
From an implementation standpoint, that user's preferred language
Operationally, that scoped application that owns tables, scripts, and configuration records
From a governance perspective, that geographic region of the data center only
explanation: Scoped applications use namespace identifiers to uniquely prefix artifacts such as tables and scripts.

prompt: Cross-scope access to another application's table or script typically requires:
choices: From an admin perspective, disabling all business rules globally under normal policy constraints
Typically, running discovery on the source scope for consistent administration
Practically speaking, an approved Restricted Caller Access privilege or configured scope access
In platform terms, deleting the target application's update set for stable outcomes
explanation: Cross-scope access is controlled through Restricted Caller Access and application scope settings.

prompt: What is a primary benefit of building automation with Flow Designer in a scoped application?
choices: Operationally, low-code flows that integrate actions, data, and logic within application boundaries
From a governance perspective, replacement of all ACLs with flow conditions
In most deployments, automatic CMDB certification without rules for governed delivery
From an implementation standpoint, elimination of all server-side scripting requirements in every scenario
explanation: Flow Designer provides visual, low-code automation that can be scoped to applications for integrations and process logic.

prompt: By default, what runtime access do new application data tables have from other scopes?
choices: Operationally, no runtime access is ever configurable under expected controls
In most deployments, restricted unless explicitly configured to allow API or web service access
In practice, fully open read/write to all global scripts in standard operations
From an implementation standpoint, open only to the email client for consistent administration
explanation: Application table runtime access is protected by default; developers configure permissions for cross-scope API calls.

prompt: A developer needs a subflow reusable across multiple flows in the same application. What should they create?
choices: In this scenario, a Flow Designer subflow that can be invoked from parent flows
Practically speaking, an import set transform map in standard operations
In platform terms, a global UI macro only for stable outcomes
From an admin perspective, a new CMDB relationship type under normal policy constraints
explanation: Subflows encapsulate reusable Flow Designer logic that parent flows can call within the application.

prompt: Which component defines a custom HTTP method, path, and script for inbound REST integration in a scoped app?
choices: In this scenario, a Scripted REST API resource
Practically speaking, a homepage gauge
In platform terms, a discovery schedule
From a governance perspective, a Transform Map
explanation: Scripted REST API resources expose endpoints backed by scripts that process requests and responses.

prompt: What is the purpose of an outbound REST message?
choices: Operationally, to call external REST APIs from server-side scripts on the instance
From an implementation standpoint, to replace GlideRecord for all queries
Typically, to discover devices on the network for consistent administration
In practice, to render client-side form layouts for governed delivery
explanation: Outbound REST messages configure HTTP calls to external systems from server-side integration code.

prompt: In an import-set integration, what does a Transform Map define?
choices: In this scenario, that Service Portal theme in standard operations under normal policy constraints
Practically speaking, how staging rows map to target records, including field mappings and optional scripts
From a governance perspective, lDAP listener ports under expected controls
In platform terms, that PA indicator timezone only for stable outcomes
explanation: Transform maps control field mapping and transformation logic from import set rows to target tables.

prompt: A developer must parse JSON from an external API response in a server script. Which class is appropriate?
choices: In most deployments, rESTMessageV2 with parsing of the response body
In this scenario, spModal in Service Portal only
From a governance perspective, glideFilter without HTTP
In platform terms, g_form on the client under expected controls
explanation: RESTMessageV2 executes outbound REST calls; the response body can be parsed in server-side script logic.

prompt: Why use an Import Set table before loading data into a target production table?
choices: Typically, to replace scripted REST APIs under normal policy constraints under expected controls
Operationally, to store client scripts for UI Builder for stable outcomes
In practice, to stage and validate source data before transform maps commit records to target tables
From an admin perspective, to bypass all ACLs permanently on the target for consistent administration
explanation: Import sets stage external data so transforms can map, validate, and load into target tables safely.
